subject: Request for Comment: FOMC March 2023 Rate Decision and Forward Guidance
body: I am a financial reporter at the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve beat. Following the FOMC's 25 basis point rate hike on March 22, 2023, I am seeking an on-record statement from the FRBSF regarding President Daly's perspective on the appropriate terminal rate given recent banking sector stress. Specifically, I would like to understand how the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank are factoring into the Committee's policy calculus. Could a spokesperson please respond by end of business Thursday ahead of our Friday publication deadline?

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when people can't afford groceries?
body: I'm a retired teacher living on a fixed income in Sacramento and I want to know why the Federal Reserve keeps making things harder for regular people. My mortgage payment didn't change but everything I buy at the store costs way more than it did two years ago and now I keep hearing about more interest rate hikes. I thought the Fed was supposed to help the economy, not make credit cards more expensive for families who are already struggling. Can someone please explain in plain language what is actually being done to help working Americans and not just banks?

subject: Inquiry Regarding Proposed Changes to Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements
body: On behalf of Western Alliance Bancorporation, we are writing to formally request clarification from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco regarding the proposed Basel III Endgame rules published in the Federal Register on September 18, 2023. Our institution has significant concerns that the proposed increase in risk-weighted asset calculations for operational risk will disproportionately burden regional banks with assets between $100 billion and $250 billion, potentially constraining our capacity to extend credit to small and medium-sized enterprises across the Western United States. We respectfully request a meeting with supervisory staff to discuss the practical implementation timeline and explore whether targeted adjustments to the standardized approach could be considered for institutions of our asset size. We look forward to engaging constructively in this rulemaking process before the January 16, 2024 comment deadline.

subject: Question about Fed balance sheet reduction and its effect on my savings account rates
body: Hi, I called because I saw something on the news about the Federal Reserve shrinking its balance sheet and I was wondering if that's connected to why my online savings account rate finally went up after years of being near zero. I have about $40,000 sitting in a high-yield savings account and I want to make sure I understand whether rates will keep going up or if the Fed might start cutting soon and I should lock in a CD rate. I'm not a financial expert but I'm trying to make smart decisions with my retirement savings and would appreciate any educational resources the Fed might have on how quantitative tightening affects deposit rates.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: Labor Market Conditions and FRBSF Research on Wage-Price Dynamics
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Senator Jacky Rosen of Nevada, Ranking Member of the Senate Banking Subcommittee on Economic Policy. The Senator's office is preparing for an upcoming hearing on the Federal Reserve's dual mandate and requests access to the most recent FRBSF research publications on wage-price spiral risks in the current tightening cycle, including any forthcoming Economic Letter analyses by Dr. Sylvain Leduc's team on labor market slack and non-linear Phillips Curve dynamics. We are particularly interested in understanding how the FRBSF's regional labor market data for the Twelfth District informs the broader FOMC assessment of whether current unemployment levels are consistent with the 2 percent inflation target over the medium term. Please direct a response to Senior Policy Advisor Maria Chen no later than two weeks prior to the scheduled March 12, 2024 hearing date.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Tightness
body: I am a staff reporter at the Wall Street Journal working on a feature examining diverging views within the Federal Reserve on the current state of labor market slack. Following President Daly's recent speech at the Peterson Institute, I would like to request an on-record comment regarding the FRBSF's assessment of whether wage growth remains inconsistent with the Fed's 2% inflation target. Specifically, I am seeking data on the San Francisco Fed's estimates of the non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment (NAIRU) and how those estimates have evolved since the post-pandemic labor market recovery. Could a spokesperson respond by end of business Friday for our Monday publication deadline?

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when people can't afford groceries?
body: I'm a retired teacher in Sacramento and I just want to understand why the Federal Reserve keeps increasing interest rates when regular people are already struggling to pay for food and rent. My mortgage payment didn't go up but my neighbor just tried to refinance and was quoted over 7% which is insane. I read that the Fed is doing this to fight inflation but it feels like the cure is worse than the disease. Can someone please explain this in plain English and tell me when rates might come back down?

subject: Concerns Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements for Regional Banks
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we are writing to formally express our significant concerns regarding the Federal Reserve Board's proposed revisions to risk-based capital requirements under the Basel III Endgame framework published in the Federal Register this past July. As a mid-sized regional bank with approximately $18 billion in total assets, we believe the proposed standardized approach for credit risk will disproportionately constrain our capacity to extend small business and agricultural loans in underserved communities throughout Northern California. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss the potential economic impact of these requirements on institutions of our size and to submit supplementary data for consideration during the public comment period. We are prepared to provide detailed loan portfolio analytics demonstrating the projected contraction in credit availability under the proposed rule.

subject: Question about Fed balance sheet reduction and Treasury market volatility
body: I am a small investor and have been following news about the Fed's quantitative tightening program where they are letting Treasury securities and mortgage-backed securities roll off the balance sheet. I am worried that this is causing volatility in the bond market and wanted to ask how much longer the Fed plans to continue shrinking its balance sheet. I also read that some economists think QT could cause another liquidity crunch like what happened in September 2019 with repo markets, and I am wondering if the Fed has safeguards in place to prevent that.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: FRBSF Economic Data on Bay Area Housing Affordability and Fed Policy
body: The Office of Representative Linda Chen (CA-17) is formally requesting economic research and data from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco regarding the relationship between the federal funds rate trajectory and housing affordability metrics in the San Francisco Bay Area and broader Northern California region. Specifically, we are seeking any FRBSF working papers, Economic Letters, or internal assessments produced between January 2022 and the present that examine how monetary policy tightening has affected home purchase mortgage originations, rental vacancy rates, and construction activity in high-cost coastal markets. This information will be used to inform upcoming subcommittee hearings on housing supply and financial conditions. We would appreciate a response within 15 business days in accordance with standard congressional correspondence protocols.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Neutral Rate Estimates
body: I am a reporter with the Wall Street Journal covering monetary policy for our economics desk. Following President Daly's recent speech at the Brookings Institution regarding the evolution of the neutral interest rate, I would like to request an official comment or clarification on how the San Francisco Fed's current r-star estimates are informing the FOMC's policy path deliberations. Specifically, I am interested in whether updated Laubach-Williams model outputs have shifted the FRBSF's internal projections ahead of the upcoming September FOMC meeting. Please respond by end of business Thursday as we are working toward a Friday publication deadline.

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when everything is so expensive already?
body: I'm a retired teacher living on a fixed income in Fresno and I genuinely don't understand why the Federal Reserve keeps making it more expensive to borrow money when groceries and rent are already through the roof. My neighbor told me higher interest rates are supposed to bring prices down but it hasn't helped my situation at all and my credit card interest shot up to 24%. Can someone please explain in plain English how this is actually supposed to help regular people and when things might start getting better?

subject: Formal Inquiry Regarding Basel III Endgame Capital Rule Impacts on Community Bank Lending
body: On behalf of Western Community Bancorp, we are writing to express our significant concerns regarding the proposed Basel III Endgame capital requirements as they pertain to institutions with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion. Our preliminary analysis suggests the proposed standardized approach for credit risk-weighted assets would require us to hold approximately 18 percent more Tier 1 capital against our current small business and agricultural loan portfolios, which would materially constrain lending capacity in underserved Central Valley communities. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss the potential economic impact assessments conducted by the Federal Reserve on mid-tier regional institutions prior to the close of the public comment period. We believe a tailored approach, consistent with the original tiering framework established under S.2155, is warranted and urge the Board to revisit the scope of application before finalizing the rule.

subject: Question about Fed balance sheet reduction and mortgage rates
body: I called in because I've been following news about the Fed selling off mortgage-backed securities and I'm trying to buy a house in the Oakland area but rates are almost 7.5 percent and it's completely out of reach. I wanted to know if the quantitative tightening program is making my mortgage rate higher than it would otherwise be and if the Fed has any plans to stop selling those bonds soon. My real estate agent mentioned something about the Fed possibly pausing QT before cutting rates but I wasn't sure if that was accurate.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: Labor Market Disparities and the Fed's Maximum Employment Mandate
body: On behalf of Congresswoman Barbara Lee's office, we are writing to formally request data and analysis from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco regarding disaggregated labor market outcomes by race and ethnicity in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. Our office is preparing for upcoming oversight hearings on whether the Federal Reserve's interpretation of its maximum employment mandate adequately accounts for persistent unemployment gaps affecting Black and Latino workers, particularly in the wake of the 2022-2024 tightening cycle. We respectfully request any available FRBSF research products, including working papers or community development analyses, that address whether recent federal funds rate increases have had disproportionate employment effects on vulnerable demographic groups. We would also welcome an opportunity to schedule a staff-level briefing with FRBSF economists prior to the hearing, tentatively scheduled for the second week of March.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Tightness
body: I am a financial correspondent at the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve beat. Following President Mary Daly's recent speech at the Commonwealth Club in San Francisco, I am seeking clarification on her characterization of the labor market as 'remarkably resilient' despite successive rate hikes. Specifically, I would like to know whether the FRBSF views the current unemployment rate of 3.9% as consistent with the Fed's longer-run neutral employment level. Could a spokesperson confirm whether President Daly will be available for a brief on-record interview before the next FOMC blackout period begins?

subject: Why are mortgage rates so high when inflation is coming down?
body: I am a first-time homebuyer in Sacramento and I am really confused about what is going on with interest rates. The news says inflation is going down toward 2% but my mortgage broker is still quoting me 7.2% on a 30-year fixed loan, which is way more than I can afford. Can someone explain why the Fed hasn't lowered rates more aggressively if prices are stabilizing? I just want to understand how this affects regular people trying to buy a home.

subject: Formal Inquiry Regarding Basel III Endgame Capital Requirement Proposals and Community Bank Impact
body: On behalf of the California Bankers Association, we are formally requesting a meeting with senior FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss the proposed Basel III Endgame rules and their disproportionate impact on mid-size and community banking institutions headquartered in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. Our members have significant concerns that the proposed increases to risk-weighted asset calculations will constrain small business lending and reduce credit availability in underserved rural communities throughout California and the Pacific Northwest. We respectfully request that the FRBSF transmit these concerns to the Board of Governors prior to the close of the public comment period and that a roundtable be scheduled no later than Q1 2024.

subject: Follow-Up on FOMC Minutes Release — QT Pace Reduction Discussion
body: This is a message from Pablo Reyes at Reuters Economics desk following today's release of the March 2024 FOMC meeting minutes. The minutes referenced internal discussion about slowing the pace of balance sheet runoff from $60 billion to approximately $30 billion per month, and I am seeking an on-the-record statement from FRBSF communications staff about how the San Francisco Fed views the timing of this quantitative tightening adjustment relative to rate cut expectations. I am on deadline for a story publishing this evening and would appreciate a callback before 4:00 PM Pacific time.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: FRBSF Economic Data Supporting Western Regional Outlook Testimony
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congressman David Huang, member of the House Financial Services Committee, in preparation for upcoming testimony by Federal Reserve officials on the economic outlook for the Western United States. The Congressman's office is requesting the most current FRBSF regional economic data, including the latest reading from the FRBSF FedViews publication and any internal assessments related to technology sector employment trends in the Bay Area following significant layoffs in 2023. We would also appreciate any FRBSF analysis on housing affordability conditions in high-cost metro areas as they relate to the Fed's financial stability mandate. Please direct your response to the committee's senior economic counsel.
sender_organization: Office of Congressman David Huang, U.S. House of Representatives

subject: Press Inquiry: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Slack at Upcoming Brookings Event
body: I am a financial correspondent with the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve beat. I understand that FRBSF President Mary Daly is scheduled to speak at the Brookings Institution next week on labor market conditions and the dual mandate. Could you confirm her prepared remarks will be released to the press ahead of the event, and whether she will be available for a brief Q&A session following her presentation? We are specifically interested in her views on whether wage growth remains a persistent driver of services inflation given the most recent CPI data showing core services ex-housing still elevated above the 2% target.

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising interest rates when people can't afford their mortgages?
body: I am a homeowner in Sacramento and I bought my house in 2021 with a 3% mortgage rate. My neighbors are now trying to refinance and the rates are over 7% which is insane. I don't understand why the Federal Reserve keeps making it so expensive to borrow money when regular people are already struggling with high grocery prices and rent. Can someone please explain in plain English what the Fed is trying to accomplish and when this will stop?

subject: Request for Supervisory Guidance on Basel III Endgame Capital Rule Implementation for Mid-Size Institutions
body: On behalf of Western Alliance Bancorporation, we respectfully request clarification from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco regarding the proposed Basel III Endgame capital requirements released by the Board of Governors in July 2023. Specifically, we seek guidance on how the expanded risk-based capital framework will apply to institutions in the $100 billion to $250 billion total asset range, and whether the operational risk capital add-ons will be phased in incrementally. Our internal modeling suggests the proposed rules could require us to raise an additional $1.2 billion in Tier 1 capital, materially affecting our lending capacity to small and medium-sized businesses in the Western region. We would welcome the opportunity to schedule a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss these implications prior to the comment period deadline.

subject: Question about Federal Reserve's balance sheet reduction and what it means for savings accounts
body: I called in because I heard on NPR that the Federal Reserve is shrinking its balance sheet through something called quantitative tightening and I want to know if that is why my savings account at my bank is finally paying more interest. I have about $40,000 sitting in a savings account that was earning almost nothing for years and now it is earning around 4.5% which is great, but I am worried about whether the Fed will cut rates soon and if I should lock into a CD instead. Is there someone at the San Francisco Fed who can give me advice on this?

subject: Congressional Inquiry: Impact of Federal Funds Rate Path on Affordable Housing Finance in the 12th District
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congresswoman Zoe Lofgren, representing California's 18th Congressional District, to the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco. We respectfully request data and analysis on how the current federal funds rate trajectory — with the target range held at 5.25% to 5.50% since the July 2023 FOMC meeting — has affected the availability and cost of financing for affordable housing development projects within the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. Community development organizations in our district have reported a significant contraction in Low Income Housing Tax Credit project financing, and we believe the FRBSF's Community Development Research team may have relevant data to inform upcoming appropriations discussions. We would appreciate a written response or briefing at your earliest convenience.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Slack
body: I'm a financial reporter with the Wall Street Journal working on a piece about diverging views within the FOMC regarding the current state of labor market slack. President Daly recently referenced the Beveridge curve in a public speech, and I'd like to request additional context or a follow-up interview regarding the FRBSF's interpretation of the quits rate as a signal for wage inflation. Could the communications office clarify whether the Bank considers current wage growth to be inconsistent with a 2% inflation target over the medium term? I'm working on a tight deadline and would need a response by end of business Thursday.

subject: Why does raising interest rates hurt regular people if it's supposed to fight inflation?
body: I don't understand why the Fed keeps raising interest rates when it just makes my mortgage and credit card bills more expensive. Inflation was already hurting me at the grocery store and now my adjustable rate mortgage went up almost two percent in one year. Can someone explain why this is supposed to help people like me and not just banks? I just want to understand what the plan is.

subject: Concerns Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements for Mid-Size Banks
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we are writing to formally express our concerns about the proposed Basel III Endgame rules released by the Federal Reserve Board in July 2023 and their disproportionate impact on institutions with assets between $100 billion and $250 billion. We believe the operational risk capital surcharges, as currently calibrated, do not accurately reflect the risk profiles of regional community-focused banks and could significantly constrain our capacity to extend credit to small and medium-sized enterprises in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. We respectfully request the FRBSF's supervisory staff engage directly with our risk management team to discuss an appropriate comment letter process and explore tailoring provisions. We have enclosed our preliminary quantitative impact assessment for your review.

subject: Question about Fed balance sheet reduction and savings account rates
body: I called in because I heard on the news that the Federal Reserve is shrinking its balance sheet and I wanted to know if that's why my savings account at my bank still pays almost nothing even though the fed funds rate is over five percent. It seems like the banks are quick to raise rates on loans but slow to raise them on deposits and I was wondering if the Fed does anything about that. My neighbor told me I should just buy Treasury bills directly but I don't know how to do that.

subject: Congressional Inquiry – FRBSF Oversight of Silicon Valley Bank Pre-Failure Supervisory Actions
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congressman Raul Vega, Ranking Member of the House Financial Services Subcommittee on Financial Institutions and Monetary Policy, regarding the FRBSF's supervisory actions toward Silicon Valley Bank in the eighteen months preceding its March 2023 failure. We are specifically requesting documentation related to any Matters Requiring Attention or Matters Requiring Immediate Attention issued to SVB between January 2022 and March 10, 2023, as well as internal communications related to the Bank's interest rate risk exposure under the Held-to-Maturity portfolio. Please be advised that this office may follow up with a formal subpoena if the requested materials are not provided within thirty days pursuant to applicable oversight authorities. We ask that all responsive records be coordinated through the FRBSF's legislative affairs office.
sender_organization: Office of Congressman Raul Vega, U.S. House of Representatives

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF's Regional Economic Conditions Ahead of November FOMC Meeting
body: I'm a financial correspondent at the Los Angeles Times preparing a piece on how the Twelfth Federal Reserve District's economic conditions are influencing the November 2023 FOMC policy deliberations. Specifically, I'd like to understand how the persistent labor market tightness in the Bay Area tech sector and elevated housing costs in San Francisco and Los Angeles are factoring into the regional Fed's assessments. Could a spokesperson or economist from the FRBSF provide on-record commentary regarding the District's contribution to the Beige Book findings? I'm on a deadline and would appreciate a response by end of business Thursday.

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when people like me can't afford anything?
body: I'm a regular person in Sacramento and I just want to understand why the Federal Reserve keeps raising interest rates when everything already costs so much. My mortgage payment jumped because of the rate hikes and now I'm barely getting by. I thought the Fed was supposed to help people, not make things more expensive. Can someone explain how making borrowing more expensive is supposed to bring down grocery prices?

subject: Concerns Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Rule Impacts on Community Banks
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we write to formally express our concerns regarding the Federal Reserve Board's proposed Basel III Endgame capital requirements and their disproportionate impact on smaller regional and community banking institutions. While we understand the objective of strengthening the financial system's resilience, the proposed risk-weight calibrations for residential mortgage portfolios and small business loans appear to inadequately account for the historically lower loss rates observed at community banks relative to the large GSIB institutions the rule was primarily designed to address. We respectfully request that the FRBSF transmit our concerns to the Board of Governors and that our institution be considered for direct consultation during the comment period. We have enclosed our preliminary quantitative impact analysis for your review.

subject: Question about quantitative tightening and whether the Fed is selling off mortgage bonds
body: I heard on a podcast that the Federal Reserve has been selling off trillions of dollars in mortgage-backed securities as part of something called quantitative tightening. I'm worried that this is going to crash the housing market in California and wanted to know if that's really happening and whether the Fed has a plan to avoid a collapse. I'd also like to know how I can find out more about the Fed's balance sheet on your website.

subject: Congressional Inquiry – Labor Market Conditions in the 12th District and Fed Employment Mandate
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congresswoman Patricia Delgado of the 18th Congressional District of California, a member of the House Financial Services Committee. The Congresswoman requests a briefing from FRBSF economists on the current state of labor market conditions across the Twelfth Federal Reserve District, with particular attention to wage growth disparities between urban and rural communities and the Fed's dual mandate obligations in the current disinflationary environment. Given recent CPI and PCE data suggesting inflation is moderating toward the 2 percent target, the Congresswoman is also seeking clarity on the timing and criteria the FOMC would consider when evaluating rate cuts. Please direct scheduling inquiries to the Committee Liaison at the office contact below.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Tightness
body: My name is Daniela Reyes and I am a financial correspondent for the Los Angeles Times. I am writing a feature on the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's perspective on labor market conditions following the November 2023 FOMC meeting. Specifically, I would like to know whether FRBSF President Mary Daly believes the current unemployment rate of 3.7% is consistent with the Fed's maximum employment mandate. Could a media relations representative please confirm her current stance and whether she anticipates any shift in the Fed's employment assessment before the December meeting?

subject: Why are interest rates so high? I can't afford my mortgage anymore
body: I'm a homeowner in Sacramento and I don't understand why the Federal Reserve keeps raising interest rates when regular people like me are struggling to keep up with our mortgage payments. My adjustable-rate mortgage just reset to over 7% and my monthly payment went up by almost $600. I thought the Fed was supposed to help the economy, not hurt working families. Is there any relief coming soon or are rates going to stay this high forever?

subject: Concerns Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements for Regional Banks
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bank, we are submitting this letter to formally express our concerns regarding the Federal Reserve's proposed Basel III Endgame rules published in July 2023. We believe that applying the advanced approaches capital framework to institutions with assets between $100 billion and $250 billion will disproportionately constrain regional banks' ability to extend credit to small and medium-sized enterprises in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. We respectfully request that FRBSF supervisory staff consider conducting an impact study specific to Western regional institutions before the comment period closes. We are available for a meeting at your earliest convenience to discuss our detailed analysis.

subject: Question about Fed's 2% inflation target and grocery prices
body: I called to ask about something I saw on the news about the Federal Reserve trying to bring inflation down to 2 percent. My grocery bill is still way higher than it was before 2020 even though they keep saying inflation is coming down. Does the 2 percent target mean prices will actually go back down to where they were, or does it just mean prices stop going up as fast? I'm retired on a fixed income and this really matters to me.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: FRBSF's Role in Monitoring Cryptocurrency Exposure at Supervised Institutions
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congressman Alan Varga, member of the House Committee on Financial Services, regarding the FRBSF's supervisory activities related to cryptocurrency asset exposure at state member banks operating within the Twelfth District. Following the collapse of several crypto-focused institutions in early 2023, the Congressman's office seeks clarification on what enhanced monitoring protocols the FRBSF has implemented and whether existing capital buffers at supervised institutions are deemed adequate to absorb potential losses from digital asset volatility. We request a written response detailing the FRBSF's current supervisory posture and any inter-agency coordination with the FDIC and OCC on this matter within 30 business days.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF Research on Wage Growth and Core PCE Divergence
body: I am a financial correspondent at the San Francisco Chronicle working on a feature examining why wage growth remains elevated even as core PCE inflation has moderated toward the Fed's 2% target. I'd like to request an on-the-record comment or interview with an FRBSF economist regarding the bank's recent working paper on labor market slack and its implications for the November FOMC decision. Could you also confirm whether President Mary Daly is available for a brief statement ahead of the October data release? My deadline is Friday at 5 PM Pacific.

subject: Why is the Fed keeping interest rates so high when my mortgage is unaffordable?
body: I've been trying to buy a home in the Bay Area for two years and rates are still over 7% on a 30-year fixed mortgage. I read that the Fed raised rates a bunch of times and now won't bring them down fast enough. I don't understand why you can't just lower rates so regular people can afford a house. Everything costs more and now I can't even get into a home — what exactly is the Fed doing to help working families like mine?

subject: Formal Inquiry Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements and Community Bank Impact
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we are writing to formally request clarification from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on the anticipated implementation timeline and scope of the Basel III Endgame capital rule revisions proposed by the Board of Governors in July 2023. Our institution is particularly concerned about the treatment of unrealized losses on held-to-maturity securities under AOCI inclusion provisions and how these rules will apply to banks with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervision staff to discuss potential compliance pathways and the comment letter submission process. We believe robust dialogue between regional Fed examiners and community banks is essential to avoiding unintended consequences for credit availability in underserved markets.

subject: Concern about Federal Reserve balance sheet reduction and retirement savings
body: I'm a retired teacher in Sacramento and I'm very worried about what's happening to my bond fund since the Fed started shrinking its balance sheet. I keep hearing terms like 'quantitative tightening' on the news and I don't understand if this means my retirement savings are in danger. Can someone at the Federal Reserve explain in plain English how QT affects regular bond investors and whether you expect to continue reducing the balance sheet through 2025?

subject: Congressional Inquiry: FRBSF Data on Regional Labor Market Conditions — District 18 Follow-Up
body: This correspondence is submitted on behalf of Congresswoman Elena Vasquez, representing California's 18th Congressional District, following her participation in the House Financial Services Committee hearing on February 14, 2025. The Congresswoman is requesting updated labor market data specific to the FRBSF's Twelfth Federal Reserve District, with particular focus on youth unemployment rates and sectoral job losses in the technology industry since the fourth quarter of 2022. We are also requesting any internal FRBSF economic assessments that address the dual mandate tradeoffs currently under consideration by the FOMC, which may have bearing on upcoming fiscal policy deliberations. Please direct your response to the Congresswoman's economic policy counsel at the address listed below.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF's Regional Economic Conditions Ahead of March FOMC Meeting
body: I am a financial correspondent with the Los Angeles Times preparing a piece on how Twelfth District economic conditions may be influencing the Federal Reserve's policy deliberations ahead of the March 2024 FOMC meeting. Specifically, I would like to understand how recent softening in West Coast labor markets and persistent services inflation in the Bay Area are being weighed against the broader national picture. Could a spokesperson or economist from the FRBSF provide on-record comment or arrange a background briefing? My deadline for the story is March 15, 2024.

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when regular people are struggling?
body: I don't understand why the Federal Reserve keeps raising interest rates when so many working families like mine are already drowning in credit card debt and can barely afford groceries. My mortgage payment went up because I have an adjustable rate, and now my car loan is also more expensive. It feels like the Fed is punishing everyday Americans just to fight inflation that was caused by supply chain problems, not by us spending too much. Can someone please explain how raising rates is actually supposed to help and when rates might start coming back down?

subject: Community Bank Concerns Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements
body: On behalf of Pacific Community Bancorp and our member institutions operating throughout California, Oregon, and Washington, we respectfully request a formal meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss the potential impact of the proposed Basel III Endgame capital rules on community bank lending capacity. Our preliminary analysis suggests that the proposed standardized approach for credit risk would significantly increase risk-weighted assets for agricultural and small business loan portfolios, directly constraining credit availability in rural Twelfth District communities. We believe the Federal Reserve Board's own quantitative impact study did not adequately capture the disproportionate burden on institutions under $100 billion in total assets. We urge the FRBSF to convey these concerns during the interagency rulemaking process and welcome the opportunity to provide supplemental data.

subject: Question about the Federal Reserve's 2% inflation target and whether it will change
body: I recently read some news articles saying that certain economists are suggesting the Fed should raise its inflation target from 2% to 3%, and I wanted to know if this is actually being considered. I'm on a fixed income and even 2% inflation eats into my purchasing power every year, so raising the target sounds really alarming to me. Is there any official process by which the public can weigh in on this kind of decision? I would appreciate any written materials the FRBSF can send me explaining how the inflation target was chosen and what it means for retirees.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: FRBSF Data on Tech Sector Layoffs and Regional Employment Outlook
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congresswoman Elena Reyes, member of the Joint Economic Committee, regarding the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's current assessment of employment conditions in the technology sector across the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. Following the significant wave of layoffs at major Silicon Valley firms beginning in late 2022, the Congresswoman's office is seeking the FRBSF's most current analysis of whether these job losses are concentrated or spreading to adjacent industries and geographic areas. We are particularly interested in any FRBSF working papers or Economic Letter publications addressing the dual mandate implications of tech-driven unemployment alongside still-elevated core PCE inflation in the region. Please respond to the committee liaison at your earliest convenience, as this information will inform upcoming testimony scheduled for September 2023.
sender_organization: Office of Congresswoman Elena Reyes, Joint Economic Committee

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF Research on Neutral Interest Rate Estimates
body: I am a financial correspondent at Bloomberg News covering the Federal Reserve's evolving assessments of the neutral rate of interest (r-star). Dr. John Williams and former FRBSF President Mary Daly have both published influential work on this topic, and I am writing a piece on how updated r-star estimates may be influencing the FOMC's current rate path deliberations. Could a spokesperson confirm whether FRBSF economists are revising their neutral rate models in light of persistent post-pandemic inflation dynamics? I am on a deadline of Friday, March 14, 2025, and would appreciate a response or on-record statement by Thursday.

subject: Why are mortgage rates still so high if the Fed cut rates?
body: I don't understand why my mortgage rate quote is still above 7% when I keep reading that the Federal Reserve cut interest rates last fall. My realtor told me to wait for rates to drop but nothing seems to have changed for regular people trying to buy a house. Can someone explain why Fed rate cuts don't automatically lower mortgage rates? I live in the Bay Area and home prices are already impossible, so this is really frustrating.

subject: Community Bank Concerns Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we respectfully submit this letter to express our concerns regarding the proposed revisions to risk-based capital requirements under the Basel III Endgame rulemaking jointly advanced by the Federal Reserve, OCC, and FDIC. While we acknowledge the importance of robust capital standards for systemically important institutions, we believe the current proposal would impose disproportionate compliance burdens on community banks with assets below $10 billion that do not pose systemic risk. Specifically, the expanded operational risk capital requirements and revised credit risk frameworks would constrain our ability to extend small business and agricultural loans in underserved communities throughout Northern California. We urge the FRBSF and the Board of Governors to consider a tiered exemption threshold that preserves the original intent of Basel III while protecting the viability of community banking institutions. We request an opportunity to present our data-driven analysis to FRBSF supervisory staff at their earliest convenience.

subject: Voicemail: Question about Fed balance sheet and quantitative tightening
body: Hi, I left a voicemail and was asked to follow up here. I've been reading about quantitative tightening and how the Fed is letting Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities roll off its balance sheet. I'm trying to understand if this is basically the opposite of the bond-buying programs they did during COVID and the 2008 crisis, and whether it's actually helping bring down inflation or just making things worse for regular investors. I listen to a lot of finance podcasts and get conflicting information, so I'd love a plain-English explanation from the actual Fed if that's possible.

subject: Congressional Inquiry — FRBSF Labor Market Data and Dual Mandate Accountability
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congressman Rafael Espinoza, member of the House Financial Services Committee, regarding the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's regional labor market research and its implications for the FOMC's dual mandate assessments. The Congressman's office is preparing for an upcoming oversight hearing and seeks the FRBSF's most recent published analysis on divergences between headline unemployment figures and broader measures of labor market slack, including the U-6 rate and labor force participation trends among prime-age workers in Western states. Additionally, the office requests clarification on how FRBSF economists are weighting employment conditions relative to the 2% inflation target in the current policy normalization cycle. Please direct responses to the Committee staff director, Ms. Alicia Fontaine, at the contact information provided, no later than ten business days from the date of this communication.
sender_organization: Office of Congressman Rafael Espinoza, House Financial Services Committee

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Softening
body: I am a reporter with the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve beat and am working on a piece about recent signals from FRBSF President Mary Daly regarding a potential softening in labor market conditions. President Daly's speech at the Brookings Institution last week appeared to suggest the Fed may be nearing a pause in its rate-hiking cycle, and I would like an official comment or clarification on her remarks. Specifically, I am asking whether her statements reflect a broader shift in the FOMC's assessment of the employment mandate versus inflation control. Could the press office respond by end of business Thursday?

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when everything is already so expensive?
body: I don't understand why the Federal Reserve keeps raising interest rates when regular people like me are already struggling to afford groceries and gas. My mortgage payment just went up because I have an adjustable rate loan and I can barely make ends meet. I heard on the news that inflation is supposed to come down but I sure don't see it at the grocery store. Can someone please explain what the Fed is actually doing to help working families and not just the banks?

subject: Inquiry Regarding Basel III Endgame Capital Rule Implications for Community Banks
body: On behalf of the California Bankers Association, we are writing to formally express our members' concerns regarding the proposed Basel III Endgame capital requirements and their disproportionate impact on community and mid-sized banks operating in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. We respectfully request that the FRBSF provide a detailed analysis of how the proposed standardized approach for credit risk-weighted assets would affect lending capacity for small business and agricultural borrowers in California and Nevada. Our members believe the current proposal as drafted could constrain credit availability in underserved markets at a particularly sensitive moment for the regional economy. We urge the FRBSF to advocate within the Federal Reserve System for a tiered implementation framework that accounts for the operational realities of institutions below $100 billion in total assets.

subject: Question about FDIC insurance and bank failures after Silicon Valley Bank collapse
body: I called because I have about $380,000 sitting in a checking account at a regional bank here in the Bay Area and after what happened with Silicon Valley Bank I am really nervous about whether my money is safe. I know the FDIC covers up to $250,000 but what about the rest of it, is that just gone if my bank fails? I also want to know if the Federal Reserve is doing anything to make sure this doesn't happen to other banks because I heard there were a lot of banks with the same kind of problems SVB had.

subject: Congressional Data Request: FRBSF Research on Quantitative Tightening and Treasury Market Liquidity
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of the Senate Banking Committee, Subcommittee on Economic Policy, in connection with upcoming oversight hearings on Federal Reserve balance sheet normalization. We are requesting any published or forthcoming FRBSF working papers or Economic Letters addressing the relationship between quantitative tightening and liquidity conditions in the U.S. Treasury market since June 2022. Of particular interest is any research examining whether the pace of balance sheet runoff has contributed to elevated bid-ask spreads or reduced depth in the Treasury market, and how the FRBSF's views on this matter compare to analysis from the Board of Governors. Please respond no later than ten business days prior to the scheduled hearing date of April 22, 2024.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Neutral Rate Estimates
body: I am a financial correspondent at the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve's evolving stance on the neutral interest rate. Following President Daly's recent public remarks suggesting the neutral rate may have shifted higher post-pandemic, I am seeking an official statement or clarification from the FRBSF communications office. Specifically, I would like to understand whether these views reflect a consensus position within the Bank or represent an individual perspective ahead of the September FOMC meeting. A response by Thursday COB would be greatly appreciated for our weekend edition.

subject: Why are mortgage rates still so high if the Fed cut rates?
body: I don't understand why my mortgage rate went up after I heard on the news that the Federal Reserve cut interest rates in September. I thought when the Fed lowers rates, everything gets cheaper to borrow. My bank is still quoting me 7.1% for a 30-year fixed loan and that feels really unfair. Can someone explain how this actually works because it seems like the Fed's decisions aren't helping regular people like me at all.

subject: Formal Comment on Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements for Regional Banks
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we respectfully submit this formal comment letter in response to the Federal Reserve's proposed revisions to the Basel III Endgame capital framework published in July 2023. Our institution strongly urges the FRBSF and the Board of Governors to reconsider the risk-weighting methodology applied to residential mortgage-backed securities held by mid-sized regional banks, as the current proposal would materially constrain our lending capacity to underserved communities in the Ninth and Twelfth Districts. We have attached an economic impact analysis prepared by our risk management team and request an opportunity to meet with supervisory staff to discuss our concerns prior to the comment deadline. We believe targeted adjustments can preserve financial stability objectives without disproportionately burdening community-focused institutions.

subject: Concern About Inflation Impact on Fixed Social Security Income
body: I am a retired schoolteacher calling to express my serious concern about how prolonged inflation is affecting people on fixed incomes like myself. Even though I received a cost-of-living adjustment to my Social Security this year, my grocery bills and utility costs have gone up far more than that adjustment covers. I want to know what the Federal Reserve is doing to bring inflation back down to 2% and how long that is expected to take. I also want someone to explain whether raising interest rates is actually hurting retired people more than it is helping us.

subject: Congressional Data Request: FRBSF Labor Market Analysis for Twelfth District
body: The Office of Representative James Wakefield of California's 14th Congressional District respectfully requests detailed labor market data and analysis prepared by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco pertaining to employment conditions in the Twelfth District for the period Q1 2022 through Q2 2024. We are particularly interested in FRBSF research on wage growth among non-college-educated workers, sectoral job gains and losses related to the technology industry correction, and any forward-looking assessments tied to the Fed's maximum employment mandate. This information will be used to inform committee testimony scheduled for October and will be handled in accordance with applicable data use guidelines. Please direct your response to our senior economic policy advisor, whose contact information is provided below.
sender_organization: Office of Representative James Wakefield, U.S. House of Representatives

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Neutral Rate Uncertainty
body: I am a reporter with the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve beat. Following President Daly's recent remarks at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research suggesting significant uncertainty around the neutral federal funds rate, I am seeking official clarification on whether the FRBSF has revised its internal estimate of r-star. Specifically, I would like to know if the Bank's economists have updated the Laubach-Williams model outputs in light of post-pandemic productivity shifts. Could a spokesperson or communications officer respond before our 5 PM ET deadline on Friday?

subject: Why is my mortgage rate still so high if the Fed cut rates?
body: I heard on the news that the Federal Reserve cut interest rates a few months ago but my bank just told me my 30-year mortgage rate is still over 7 percent. I thought when the Fed cuts rates everything gets cheaper. Can someone explain why this is happening and when regular people like me will actually see lower rates on our loans? This is really frustrating and I just want to understand how the system works.

subject: Concerns Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements Impact on Community Banks
body: On behalf of the California Bankers Association, we write to formally express our members' concerns regarding the Federal Reserve's proposed revisions to risk-based capital requirements under the Basel III Endgame framework. Our analysis indicates that the operational risk capital add-ons disproportionately affect mid-tier and community institutions that rely heavily on fee-based income from mortgage servicing and wealth management activities. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervision staff to discuss potential tiering adjustments and to provide data on projected capital shortfalls across our membership. We believe a tailored approach is essential to preserving lending capacity in underserved California markets.

subject: Caller Inquiry: Impact of Fed Policy on Grocery Prices
body: A caller identified themselves as a retired schoolteacher from Fresno who said they are very concerned that grocery prices have not come down despite the Federal Reserve claiming inflation is under control. The caller asked whether the Fed's 2 percent inflation target means prices will actually fall or just stop rising so fast, and expressed frustration that their fixed Social Security income is not keeping pace with the cost of living. The caller specifically referenced a recent FOMC statement they had read online and wanted to know why the Fed is not doing more to bring prices back to where they were in 2020.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: FRBSF Assessment of Labor Market Softening in the Twelfth District
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Senator Alex Padilla's office, Senate Banking Committee staff, in advance of the upcoming semiannual Monetary Policy Report hearing. We are requesting the FRBSF's most current assessment of labor market conditions across the Twelfth Federal Reserve District, with particular attention to divergences between coastal metropolitan areas and inland regions of California. The senator is specifically interested in whether recent upticks in unemployment insurance claims in the Central Valley reflect cyclical softening tied to Fed rate policy or structural factors such as agricultural automation. Please provide any available District-level data or Beige Book supplementary analysis at your earliest convenience.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Tightness
body: I am a staff reporter at the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve beat. Following President Mary Daly's recent speech at the Brookings Institution, I am seeking clarification on the FRBSF's current assessment of labor market slack and its implications for the pace of future rate adjustments. Specifically, I would like to understand whether the Bank views the current unemployment rate of 4.1% as consistent with the long-run neutral level. Could a spokesperson or communications officer respond before our 5 PM Eastern deadline on Friday?

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when people can't afford their mortgages?
body: I just want someone to explain to me why the Federal Reserve keeps making it harder for regular people to buy a home. My mortgage rate went from 3.2% to over 7% and I had to back out of purchasing a house because of these rate hikes. I understand inflation is a problem but it feels like the cure is worse than the disease for working families like mine. Is there any relief coming soon or should I just give up on homeownership entirely?

subject: Formal Inquiry Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements for Regional Banks
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, I am writing to formally express our institution's concerns regarding the Federal Reserve's proposed revisions to risk-based capital requirements under the Basel III Endgame framework. Our analysis indicates that the proposed standardized approach for operational risk would impose a disproportionate capital surcharge on mid-sized institutions with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion that do not engage in the complex trading activities these rules were designed to address. We respectfully request the opportunity to present our findings to FRBSF supervisory staff and ask that the comment period be extended to allow adequate time for community and regional banks to conduct thorough impact assessments. We appreciate the Federal Reserve's commitment to maintaining a resilient banking system while preserving the credit intermediation capacity of community lenders.

subject: Concern About Inflation Still Being Too High Despite Fed Actions
body: I called today because I'm very frustrated that grocery prices and rent are still way up even though I keep hearing the Fed has been fighting inflation for over a year. My Social Security cost-of-living adjustment didn't even come close to covering what I'm actually paying more at the store. I saw the latest CPI report showed inflation at 3.7% which is still way above the 2% target and I want to know when things are going to actually get back to normal for retired people on fixed incomes.

subject: Congressional Data Request: FRBSF Quantitative Tightening Balance Sheet Projections
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of the Senate Banking Committee's Democratic staff in support of an oversight review of the Federal Reserve's balance sheet normalization program. We are requesting any publicly available FRBSF research or internal projections related to the pace of quantitative tightening, specifically the expected timeline for reserves to reach the ample-reserves threshold and the potential spillover effects on short-term funding markets such as repo and federal funds. Please also indicate whether FRBSF economists have modeled scenarios in which QT interacts with ongoing FOMC rate decisions to produce unintended tightening in financial conditions beyond what is signaled through the federal funds rate target. We would appreciate a response or referral to the appropriate Federal Reserve Board staff within ten business days.

subject: Press Inquiry: FRBSF President's Remarks on Neutral Rate Estimates
body: Hi, I'm a reporter with Bloomberg covering the Fed beat and I'm reaching out ahead of the upcoming FOMC meeting scheduled for late July 2023. FRBSF President Mary Daly made comments last week suggesting the neutral rate may have shifted higher than pre-pandemic estimates, and I'd like to request a follow-up interview or an official clarifying statement. Specifically, I'd like to understand whether the San Francisco Fed's internal models now place r-star above 2.5 percent. Could your communications team confirm availability for a 20-minute call before the blackout period begins?

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when people can't afford groceries?
body: I'm a retired teacher in Sacramento and I just want to understand why the Federal Reserve keeps raising interest rates when everyday people are already struggling to afford basic necessities. My fixed income hasn't gone up but my credit card interest rate jumped from 18% to almost 25% in the past year. I read that inflation is supposed to come down because of these hikes but I'm not seeing it at the grocery store. Can someone please explain in plain language how this is supposed to help regular Americans?

subject: Request for Supervisory Guidance on Commercial Real Estate Concentration Risk
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bank, we are writing to the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco to request updated supervisory guidance regarding concentration risk thresholds for commercial real estate loan portfolios in light of rising office vacancy rates across major West Coast markets. Our current CRE-to-capital ratio stands at 287 percent, and while this remains below the 300 percent threshold outlined in existing interagency guidance, we are uncertain how examiners will treat our downtown San Francisco office exposure given recent market deterioration. We respectfully request a pre-examination meeting with our dedicated supervisory team to discuss these concerns before our scheduled full-scope examination in Q4 2024. A written response or scheduled call would be greatly appreciated.

subject: Concern About Job Losses and Fed's Employment Mandate
body: A caller identified as a software engineer recently laid off in the Bay Area wanted to express concern that the Federal Reserve's aggressive rate hiking cycle has contributed to widespread tech sector layoffs by tightening credit conditions for growth-stage companies. He noted that the Fed has a dual mandate including maximum employment, and he questioned whether policymakers are giving sufficient weight to the employment side of that mandate versus inflation control. The caller asked whether there is a public comment mechanism to share his perspective with FOMC members before the next policy decision.

subject: Congressional Inquiry Regarding FRBSF Oversight of SVB Prior to March 2023 Failure
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Senator Elizabeth Warren's office and relates to the Senate Banking Committee's ongoing investigation into the supervisory failures preceding the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank in March 2023. We are requesting all available documentation, including examination reports, supervisory letters, and internal escalation records, pertaining to the FRBSF's oversight of SVB from January 2020 through March 10, 2023. We note that the Fed's own post-mortem report acknowledged supervisory shortcomings and we would like to schedule a briefing with senior FRBSF examination staff no later than 30 days from the date of this correspondence. Please direct your response to the Senate Banking Committee minority counsel at the address below.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Slack at Jackson Hole
body: I am a financial reporter at the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve beat. Following remarks made by FRBSF President Mary Daly at the Jackson Hole Economic Symposium regarding residual labor market slack and its implications for the neutral rate, I would like to request an on-record clarification on whether the Bank views current unemployment levels as consistent with the FOMC's maximum employment mandate. Specifically, I am interested in whether the FRBSF's internal estimates of the non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment (NAIRU) have been revised since the September 2023 Summary of Economic Projections. Could you please connect me with the appropriate communications officer at your earliest convenience?

subject: Why are mortgage rates so high if you're supposed to help regular people?
body: I've been trying to buy a home for the past two years and every time I get close, mortgage rates go up again because of what the Fed does. I don't understand how raising the federal funds rate to over 5% is helping anyone except the banks. My family has good credit and steady jobs but we still can't afford a house in the Bay Area because rates are pushing monthly payments way out of reach. Can someone explain to me why the Fed keeps doing this when regular working people are getting hurt?

subject: Formal Comment on Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Rule Changes – Community Bank Impact Assessment
body: On behalf of the Western Independent Bankers Association, we write to formally express our concerns regarding the proposed revisions to risk-based capital requirements under the Basel III Endgame framework as jointly published by the Federal Reserve, OCC, and FDIC. Our member institutions, which predominantly serve agricultural and small business lending markets across the Twelfth Federal Reserve District, project that the proposed standardized approach for credit risk would increase required capital buffers by an estimated 12–18% for portfolios heavily weighted toward commercial real estate and agricultural loans. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to present our quantitative analysis and discuss potential tiering accommodations for institutions below the $100 billion asset threshold. We have enclosed a detailed impact study prepared by our chief economist for your review.

subject: Concern About Inflation Still Being Too High Despite Fed Rate Hikes
body: The caller stated that she has noticed grocery prices and gas have not come down despite hearing news that the Fed has been raising rates for over a year. She wanted to know when the Fed expects inflation to return to the 2% target and whether the rate hikes are actually working. She expressed frustration that her Social Security income has not kept pace with price increases and asked if the Fed considers the impact on fixed-income retirees when making decisions.

subject: Congressional Inquiry – FRBSF Exposure to Silicon Valley Bank Pre-Failure Supervisory Actions
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congressman Alan Trevino, Ranking Member of the House Financial Services Subcommittee on Financial Institutions and Monetary Policy, as part of the subcommittee's ongoing review of the March 2023 banking stress events. We are requesting documentation and a staff-level briefing regarding the supervisory timeline and examination findings the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco maintained for Silicon Valley Bank in the 18 months preceding its failure on March 10, 2023. Of particular interest are any Matters Requiring Attention (MRAs) or Matters Requiring Immediate Attention (MRIAs) issued during that period, and the Bank's internal escalation protocols for interest rate risk in the held-to-maturity securities portfolio. Please acknowledge receipt and provide a target date for response consistent with the subcommittee's oversight timeline.
sender_organization: Office of Congressman Alan Trevino, U.S. House of Representatives

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Slack
body: I am a financial reporter at the San Francisco Chronicle covering the Fed's latest commentary on labor market conditions. During last week's economic symposium, President Daly referenced 'residual slack' in the labor market despite headline unemployment near historic lows. Could the FRBSF press office clarify whether this characterization aligns with the broader FOMC consensus heading into the March 2024 policy meeting? We are on deadline and would appreciate a response by end of business Thursday.

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when people can't afford groceries?
body: I'm a retired schoolteacher living on a fixed income and I don't understand why the Federal Reserve keeps making it harder for regular people. My mortgage payment went up because of the rate hikes and now groceries and gas are still expensive anyway. It seems like the Fed is just hurting working people to help Wall Street. Can someone explain in plain English why this is supposed to help inflation when everything still costs so much?

subject: Concerns Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements for Regional Banks
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bank, we write to express significant concerns about the proposed implementation of Basel III Endgame capital rules as released by the Federal Reserve Board in July 2023. We believe the proposed risk-weighted asset calculations disproportionately burden community and mid-sized regional institutions that do not pose systemic risk, potentially constraining small business lending in underserved markets throughout Northern California. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss these implications and submit formal comments prior to the public comment deadline. We have attached our preliminary impact analysis for your review.

subject: Question about Fed balance sheet reduction and Treasury market volatility
body: I'm a small investor and I've been reading about quantitative tightening and how the Fed is shrinking its balance sheet by letting Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities roll off. I want to understand whether this runoff is contributing to the volatility I'm seeing in the 10-year Treasury yield lately. Is there a published schedule or explanation of how the monthly caps work that I could access on your website?

subject: Congressional Inquiry: FRBSF Economic Assessment of AI-Driven Productivity and Inflation Trajectory
body: The office of Congresswoman Zoe Lofgren is preparing materials for an upcoming hearing on artificial intelligence and macroeconomic policy. We are requesting any publicly available or releasable research from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on how AI-driven productivity gains may influence the Federal Reserve's long-run neutral rate estimates and its 2 percent inflation target framework. Specifically, we are interested in whether FRBSF economists anticipate that technology-led supply-side improvements could allow the FOMC to achieve its dual mandate at a lower federal funds rate than currently projected. Please direct a response to our policy director at your earliest convenience.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF's Regional Economic Conditions Ahead of March FOMC Meeting
body: Hi, I'm a financial reporter at the San Francisco Chronicle working on a piece about how economic conditions in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District may be influencing deliberations ahead of the March 2024 FOMC meeting. Specifically, I'm interested in whether slowing tech-sector employment in the Bay Area and cooling housing markets in California are being factored into the Fed's broader assessment of labor market tightness. Could a representative from FRBSF's public affairs or research team provide comment or schedule a brief interview? My deadline is this Friday at 5 PM PT.

subject: Why are interest rates so high? I can't afford a mortgage anymore
body: I've been trying to buy a house for over a year now and every time I get close, mortgage rates go up again. I heard the Federal Reserve is the one raising rates and I just don't understand why they keep doing this when regular people like me are getting hurt. I make decent money but a 7% mortgage rate means I literally cannot qualify for a home in my area. Is there any plan to bring rates down soon or should I just give up?

subject: Inquiry Regarding Proposed Changes to Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements for Regional Banks
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we write to formally request clarification regarding the Federal Reserve Board's proposed revisions to the Basel III Endgame capital framework and their anticipated impact on mid-sized regional institutions with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion. We are particularly concerned about the operational risk capital add-on methodology and how it will affect our lending capacity to small and medium-sized enterprises throughout Northern California. Our risk management team has modeled several scenarios and believes the proposed rules as written could reduce our commercial loan portfolio by an estimated 12 to 15 percent. We respectfully request a meeting with supervisory staff at the FRBSF to discuss our concerns before the public comment period closes.

subject: Caller Question About Federal Reserve's Role in Controlling Grocery Prices
body: A member of the public called to ask why the Federal Reserve hasn't done more to bring down grocery prices, stating that food costs have risen dramatically over the past two years at their local supermarket. The caller expressed frustration that despite hearing news reports about inflation coming down, they personally don't see any relief at the checkout line. They asked whether the Fed's 2 percent inflation target means prices will actually go back down to where they were in 2020 or just stop rising as fast.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: Federal Reserve's Assessment of Unemployment Trends in High-Cost Western States
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congressman Rafael Espinoza of California's 18th Congressional District. The Congressman's office has received a significant volume of constituent correspondence regarding rising unemployment in the semiconductor and clean-energy manufacturing sectors across Silicon Valley and the Central Valley. We are requesting FRBSF's most recent assessment of labor market conditions in these industries and whether the current stance of monetary policy is contributing to job losses in rate-sensitive capital-intensive sectors. Additionally, we would like to understand how FRBSF economists are weighing the maximum employment mandate against ongoing inflation reduction efforts as the Fed considers the pace of future rate adjustments. Please direct your response to the Congressman's senior economic policy advisor at your earliest convenience.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Tightness
body: I am a financial correspondent at the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve's dual mandate. Following FRBSF President Mary Daly's recent remarks at the Peterson Institute suggesting the labor market remains 'in a good place,' I am seeking clarification on whether the Bank views current nonfarm payroll growth as consistent with a soft landing scenario. Specifically, I'd like to understand how the FRBSF reconciles strong job creation with the FOMC's stated intention to hold rates higher for longer. A response by end of business Friday would be appreciated for our weekend edition.

subject: Why is the Fed keeping rates so high when I can't afford my mortgage?
body: I bought my house in 2019 at a 3.1% rate and now I'm trying to refinance after a job change but rates are above 7% and it's completely impossible. I keep hearing the Fed is trying to fight inflation but my grocery bills are still sky high AND now I can't even think about moving. Can someone explain why you can't just lower rates back down? It feels like regular people are paying the price for problems we didn't cause.

subject: Community Bank Comment on Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we respectfully submit this letter to express significant concern regarding the Basel III Endgame capital adequacy proposals as advanced by the Federal Reserve Board and the prudential regulators. We believe the proposed increase in risk-weighted asset calculations for residential mortgage portfolios will disproportionately burden community institutions that did not contribute to the systemic risks these rules are designed to address. Our analysis indicates that compliance would require us to raise Tier 1 capital by approximately 18%, potentially forcing a curtailment of small business lending in underserved rural communities throughout California's Central Valley. We urge the FRBSF to advocate within the Federal Reserve System for a tiered implementation framework that exempts institutions below $10 billion in total assets.

subject: Question about Fed balance sheet reduction and its effect on savings account rates
body: A caller asked why, if the Fed is doing quantitative tightening and selling off Treasury securities, her online savings account is only paying 0.01% interest at her local bank when she reads that the federal funds rate is over 5%. She mentioned feeling confused by news reports about the Fed's balance sheet shrinking from nearly $9 trillion and wanted to know if QT would eventually help regular savers see better deposit rates. The caller also asked whether there was a consumer resource at the FRBSF that could explain how Fed policy affects deposit rates in plain language.

subject: Congressional Inquiry – FRBSF Economic Outlook Data for Upcoming Hearing on Regional Financial Stability
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congresswoman Linda Torres, Ranking Member of the House Financial Services Subcommittee on Monetary Policy and Trade. The Subcommittee is preparing for an oversight hearing scheduled for September 2024 focused on financial stability risks in the Western United States, including commercial real estate exposure among mid-size banks and the impact of elevated interest rates on regional credit conditions. We respectfully request any published or forthcoming FRBSF research, including relevant FRBSF Economic Letters or working papers, addressing CRE loan concentration risks, deposit outflow trends since March 2023, and the FRBSF's assessment of tail risks to the Twelfth District banking sector. We would also welcome a staff briefing prior to the hearing if the Bank is able to accommodate that request.
sender_organization: Office of Congresswoman Linda Torres, U.S. House of Representatives
